subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 3! In this unit you will learn how to make sense of data – the collections of numbers and facts we meet every single day. When Amina compares the ratings of two books before choosing one, when Hassan looks at the football scores from last weekend, or when Khadija checks how much rain fell each day before the family plants their maize near Garissa, they are all working with data. Averages are the powerful tools that turn a long, confusing list of numbers into one clear, typical value you can actually use.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 6 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: This unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, work through every example yourself on paper, and only move on when you feel confident. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you fresh practice questions, explain a tricky step in a new way, or check your working. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that still feels difficult.

Why does this matter so much? We live in a world flooded with numbers. Shopkeepers in the Hargeisa market track their busiest hours; news reports quote average prices; sports commentators quote a player’s average score; weather services report average temperatures for planning trips between Mogadishu and Nairobi. People who understand averages can read these numbers wisely and make good decisions. People who do not can be misled – sometimes by accident, and sometimes on purpose. Learning to be a careful, critical thinker about data is one of the most useful skills mathematics can give you.

As you work, keep a pencil, some paper, and a calculator beside you. A calculator is allowed for the adding and dividing so that you can concentrate on the ideas – but you must still understand every step, because a calculator will happily give you a wrong answer if you press the wrong buttons. Mathematics is not a subject you learn only by reading; you must do it. If you get an answer wrong, that is not a failure – it simply shows you exactly what to practise next.
Find the mode – the most common value – and recognise data that is bimodal (has two modes) or has no mode.
Find the median – the middle value when the data is put in order – for both odd and even numbers of values.
Calculate the mean by adding all the values and dividing by how many there are.
Calculate the range – the difference between the highest and lowest values – and use it to describe how consistent data is.
Compare two sets of data using averages and range together.
Choose the most appropriate average for a particular situation and explain your choice.
Think critically about statistics and spot when an average is being used to mislead.

concepts.title: What Is an Average?
concepts.explanation: An average is a single number that represents what is typical or normal for a whole group of numbers. Instead of trying to hold twenty different scores in your head at once, an average lets you say, “on the whole, this is what the data looks like.” It is like describing a whole class of children with one sensible sentence rather than listing every child separately. There are three main types of average, and this is the first surprise for many learners: the word “average” does not mean only one thing. The three…
concepts.example: Mode – the value that appears most often (the most popular value).

concepts.title: The Mode – Finding What Is Most Common
concepts.explanation: The mode is the value that appears most often in a set of data. It is the most popular, most frequent value. To find it you do not need to add or divide anything – you simply count how many times each value appears and pick the one that appears the most. Look carefully at every value in your data.
concepts.example: Count how many times each different value appears (a frequency table helps).

concepts.title: The Median – Finding the Middle Value
concepts.explanation: The median is the middle value when all of the data has been arranged in order from smallest to largest. The single most important rule of the median is this: you must put the data in order first. If you try to find the middle of an unordered list, you will almost always get the wrong answer. Ordering first is a habit you must never skip. Finding the median when there is an odd number of values. Here the method is simple:
concepts.example: Arrange all the values in order from smallest to largest.

concepts.title: The Mean – Sharing Out Equally
concepts.explanation: The mean is what most people are thinking of when they say the word “average.” It is the total of all the values divided by how many values there are. A good way to picture the mean is fair sharing: if you gathered up everything and shared it out so that every share was equal, the size of one equal share is the mean. Here is the three-step method – add, count, divide:
concepts.example: Add up all the values to get the total.

concepts.title: The Range – Measuring the Spread
concepts.explanation: The range is not an average. Instead of telling us a typical value, it tells us how spread out the data is – how far apart the biggest and smallest values are. The range is the difference between the highest and lowest values. To find it: Example 1 – daily temperatures. Over five days near Kismaayo the midday temperatures were 15°C, 19°C, 23°C, 19°C, 16°C. The highest is 23°C and the lowest is 15°C, so the range is 23 − 15 = 8°C.
concepts.example: Example 2 – consistency in practice. Two girls record their daily football-practice times in minutes. Sahra’s times are 10, 8, 2, 10, 20 – highest 20, lowest 2, so her range is 20 − 2 = 18 minutes. Hani’s times are 10,…

concepts.title: Comparing Data and Choosing the Right Average
concepts.explanation: Now we bring everything together. When two sets of data are compared, looking at an average and the range together often reveals things that neither shows alone. Example – two footballers. Two players each score in six matches. Player A scores 12, 12, 13, 13, 14, 14 goals across a season’s games (mean of about 13). Player B scores 8, 10, 13, 14, 16, 19 (also a mean of about 13). Their means are the same, so on paper they look equal. But Player A’s range is 14 − 12 = 2, while Player B’s range is 19 − 8 = 11. Player…
concepts.example: So which average should you use? Different situations call for different averages. Here is a clear guide:

methods.title: How to find the mean
steps: Add up all the values to get the total.
Count how many values there are.
Divide the total by the count.
Check the answer lies between the lowest and highest values.

methods.title: How to find the median
steps: Write the values in order from smallest to largest.
If the count is odd, pick the single middle value.
If the count is even, average the two middle values.

methods.title: How to choose the right average
steps: For the most popular item or a category, use the mode.
If there is an outlier or extreme value, use the median.
If the values are similar and you want every value counted, use the mean.

methods.title: Median of an even set
methods.example: A basketball player scores 14, 18, 22, 16, 20, 16 in six games. Find the mode, median and mean.
steps: Solution: Mode: 16 appears twice, more than any other, so the mode is 16.
Median: order them – 14, 16, 16, 18, 20, 22.
With six values the two middle ones are the 3rd and 4th: 16 and 18.

methods.title: Find and use the range
methods.example: Two archers score: Archer A – 7, 8, 7, 8, 7, 8, 7; Archer B – 4, 9, 6, 10, 5, 11, 6. Find each mean and range, and say who is more consistent.
steps: Solution: Archer A total = 52, mean = 52 ÷ 7 ≈ 7.4; range = 8 − 7 = 1.
Archer B total = 51, mean = 51 ÷ 7 ≈ 7.3; range = 11 − 4 = 7.
Their means are almost the same, but Archer A’s range is far smaller, so Archer A is much more consistent.

methods.title: Work backwards from the mean
methods.example: The mean of 5 numbers is 24. Four of them are 20, 22, 26 and 28. Find the fifth.
steps: Solution: Total needed = mean × count = 24 × 5 = 120.
The four known numbers add to 20 + 22 + 26 + 28 = 96.
So the fifth number = 120 − 96 = 24.

workedExamples.title: Find the mode
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Count each value. 5 appears three times, 8 appears twice, 12 and 10 appear once each. The value appearing most often is 5, so the mode is 5.

workedExamples.title: Find the median of an odd set
workedExamples.solution: Solution: First order the data: 8, 11, 12, 15, 16, 19, 23. There are 7 values, so the middle one is the 4th value, which is 15. The median is 15.

workedExamples.title: Find the mean
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Add: 6 + 9 + 12 + 15 + 18 = 60. Count: there are 5 values. Divide: 60 ÷ 5 = 12. The mean is 12. (Check: 12 lies between the lowest, 6, and the highest, 18 – sensible.)

workedExamples.title: Median of an even set
workedExamples.prompt: A basketball player scores 14, 18, 22, 16, 20, 16 in six games. Find the mode, median and mean.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Mode: 16 appears twice, more than any other, so the mode is 16. Median: order them – 14, 16, 16, 18, 20, 22. With six values the two middle ones are the 3rd and 4th: 16 and 18. Median = (16 + 18) ÷ 2 = 17. Mean: total = 14+18+22+16+20+16 = 106; 106 ÷ 6 = 17.7 (to one decimal place).

workedExamples.title: Find and use the range
workedExamples.prompt: Two archers score: Archer A – 7, 8, 7, 8, 7, 8, 7; Archer B – 4, 9, 6, 10, 5, 11, 6. Find each mean and range, and say who is more consistent.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Archer A total = 52, mean = 52 ÷ 7 ≈ 7.4; range = 8 − 7 = 1. Archer B total = 51, mean = 51 ÷ 7 ≈ 7.3; range = 11 − 4 = 7. Their means are almost the same, but Archer A’s range is far smaller, so Archer A is much more consistent.

workedExamples.title: Work backwards from the mean
workedExamples.prompt: The mean of 5 numbers is 24. Four of them are 20, 22, 26 and 28. Find the fifth.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Total needed = mean × count = 24 × 5 = 120. The four known numbers add to 20 + 22 + 26 + 28 = 96. So the fifth number = 120 − 96 = 24.

workedExamples.title: Choose the right average
workedExamples.prompt: A data set is 5, 7, 8, 8, 9, 25. Find the mode, median and mean, and decide which best represents a typical value.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Mode = 8 (appears twice). Median: already ordered, two middle values are 8 and 8, so median = 8. Mean: total = 62, 62 ÷ 6 ≈ 10.3. The value 25 is an outlier that has pulled the mean up to 10.3, well above most of the data. So the median (or mode), 8, best represents the typical value here, because the mean is distorted by the outlier.

workedExamples.title: Compare with mean and range
workedExamples.prompt: Two classes take the same test. Class A: mean 78, range 15. Class B: mean 78, range 35. Which class was more consistent, and which might have more pupils needing extra help?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Both classes have the same mean, so on average they did equally well. But Class A has the smaller range (15), so it was more consistent – pupils scored close together. Class B’s larger range (35) means scores were more spread out, so Class B is more likely to have some pupils who scored very low and need extra help.

workedExamples.title: Practice 9
workedExamples.prompt: A class has scores with a mean of 75 and a range of 30. If the lowest score is 60, find the highest score.

workedExamples.title: Practice 10
workedExamples.prompt: For 10, 12, 12, 15, 15, 15, 18, 25 find the mode, median and mean. Which best represents the typical value, and why?
workedExamples.solution: Mode 15, median 15, mean 122÷8 = 15.25; all are close, so any is reasonable, but the median/mode 15 nicely represents the typical value.

workedExamples.title: Practice 11
workedExamples.prompt: Create two data sets of 5 numbers that both have a mean of 10 – one with a small range, one with a large range.

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: A data set of 10 values has a mean of 50. Nine of the values are 45, 47, 48, 49, 51, 52, 53, 54, 55. Find the tenth value.
workedExamples.solution: 1) Total must be 10 × 50 = 500; the nine given add to 454; tenth = 500 − 454 = 46.

practice.prompt: A student’s test scores are 85, 92, 78, 88, 82. Find the mean. What score is needed on a 6th test to raise the mean to 87?
practice.answer: Mean of five = 425 ÷ 5 = 85; for a mean of 87 over six tests total must be 522, so the 6th score = 522 − 425 = 97.
practice.hint: Represent the information, name the rule, then solve one step at a time.

activities.title: Family Data Detective
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and your family members.
steps: Choose something to measure: the ages, heights, or shoe sizes of everyone in your household.
Write down every value in a neat list.
Find the mode, the median, the mean and the range of your data.
Talk about it: which average best describes your family? Is anyone an outlier?
Keep your results – you can compare them with a friend’s family and see whose data is more spread out.

activities.title: The Weather Tracker Investigation
activities.materials: a way to note the temperature (a thermometer, a phone, or a weather report) and a notebook.
steps: For one whole week, record the midday temperature each day.
At the end of the week, find the mean temperature and the range.
Was the week warm or cool overall (mean)? Was it steady or changeable (range)?
Predict next week’s pattern, then track it and see how close you were.

activities.title: The Dice Mean Game
activities.materials: one or two dice (or numbers 1–6 written on folded paper to draw from).
steps: Each player rolls the dice five times and writes down the five numbers.
Everyone calculates the mean of their own five rolls.
The player with the highest mean wins the round.
Play several rounds. Does anyone’s mean stay close to 3.5 – the mean you expect from a fair die?
This game shows how the mean settles near a predictable value the more you play.

activities.title: Market Stall Stock Planner (Mode in Action)
activities.materials: paper and pencil, and your imagination as a shopkeeper.
steps: Pretend you run a sambusa stall. Over ten days you sell these numbers of trays: 3, 5, 4, 5, 6, 5, 4, 5, 7, 5.
Find the mode – the number of trays you sell most often.
Use the mode to decide how many trays to prepare on a typical day. Why is the mode better than the mean here?
Now invent your own ten days of sales and find the mode again.

activities.title: The Misleading Statistic Challenge (Family or Group Task)
activities.materials: paper, pencil, and one or more other people.
steps: Together, invent a small set of about eight numbers that includes one big outlier – for example, pocket money amounts.
Each person calculates the mode, median and mean.
Now play ‘persuade’: one person argues using the average that makes the group look richest, another argues using the average that looks poorest.
Discuss: which average was the most honest, and why can the same data tell different stories?

activities.title: Sports Statistician
activities.materials: scores from any game or sport – real football results, or a game you play at home.
steps: Record the points or goals scored across at least six matches by two players or teams.
For each, calculate the mean and the range.
Decide who is the higher scorer (mean) and who is the more consistent scorer (range).
Write one sentence recommending which player you would pick for your team, and why.

After your activities, talk or write about these questions: Which activity helped you understand the difference between the averages best? When did an outlier change the answer? Which measure – mode, median, mean or range – do you now feel most confident with, and which needs more practice? Share your answers with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: The median is the middle value when
rules.text: The median is the middle value when all of the data has been arranged in order from smallest to largest. The single most important rule of the median is this: you must put the data in order first. If you try to find the middle of an unordered list, you will almost always get the wrong answer. Ordering first is a habit you must never skip.

terms: A collection of numbers or facts
A value far from the rest of the data
Values close together; a small range
commonMistakes: Always write the ordered list first
Thinking the mode is the biggest number
Count how often each value appears
Letting an outlier fool the mean
Check for outliers; use the median instead
Reporting an average with no spread
Always give the range as well

realProblems.prompt: A company tracks daily sales for two weeks. Week 1: 1200, 1250, 1180, 1220, 1240, 1200, 1230 shillings. Week 2: 1000, 1400, 1100, 1350, 1050, 1380, 1120. Find each week’s range and say which week had more consistent sales.
realProblems.answer: 1) Week 1 range = 1250 − 1180 = 70; Week 2 range = 1400 − 1000 = 400 → Week 1 was far more consistent.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1) Week 1 range = 1250 − 1180 = 70; Week 2 range = 1400 − 1000 = 400 → Week 1 was far more consistent.

realProblems.prompt: A sports commentator says a player is “very consistent, averaging 15 points a game.” The player’s last 10 scores were 0, 0, 2, 5, 8, 12, 18, 25, 35, 45. Find the mean and the range. Is the claim fair? Suggest a better description.
realProblems.answer: Mean = 150 ÷ 10 = 15, but range = 45 − 0 = 45; the claim is not fair – the scores swing wildly, so the player is very inconsistent despite a mean of 15.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Mean = 150 ÷ 10 = 15, but range = 45 − 0 = 45; the claim is not fair – the scores swing wildly, so the player is very inconsistent despite a mean of 15.

realProblems.prompt: A company advertises, “Our average customer rating is 4.2 out of 5!” The actual ratings are 5, 5, 5, 5, 5, 1, 1, 1, 1, 2. Check the mean, find the median, and say whether the advert is misleading.
realProblems.answer: Mean = 31 ÷ 10 = 3.1, not 4.2, so the claim is mathematically wrong; ordered 1,1,1,1,2,5,5,5,5,5 → median = (2+5)÷2 = 3.5. The advert is misleading because most ratings are low.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Mean = 31 ÷ 10 = 3.1, not 4.2, so the claim is mathematically wrong; ordered 1,1,1,1,2,5,5,5,5,5 → median = (2+5)÷2 = 3.5. The advert is misleading because most ratings are low.

realProblems.prompt: Two cities each report a mean temperature of 22°C. City A has a range of 15°C; City B has a range of 35°C. If you prefer stable weather, which city would you choose, and why?
realProblems.answer: Choose City A: same mean but a smaller range (15°C vs 35°C) means steadier, more stable weather.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Choose City A: same mean but a smaller range (15°C vs 35°C) means steadier, more stable weather.

realProblems.prompt: A factory measures product lengths (cm): 9.8, 10.1, 9.9, 10.0, 10.2, 9.7, 10.1, 9.9, 10.0, 10.3. Find the mode, median, mean and range, and decide whether the process is consistent.
realProblems.answer: Mode 10.1 (and 9.9, 10.0 also twice – accept 10.0/10.1); median = (10.0+10.0)÷2 = 10.0; mean = 99.9 ÷ 10 ≈ 9.99; range = 10.3 − 9.7 = 0.6. The very small range shows the process is consistent.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Mode 10.1 (and 9.9, 10.0 also twice – accept 10.0/10.1); median = (10.0+10.0)÷2 = 10.0; mean = 99.9 ÷ 10 ≈ 9.99; range = 10.3 − 9.7 = 0.6. The very small range shows the process is consistent.

realProblems.prompt: A data set of 10 values has a mean of 50. Nine of the values are 45, 47, 48, 49, 51, 52, 53, 54, 55. Find the tenth value.
realProblems.answer: 1) Total must be 10 × 50 = 500; the nine given add to 454; tenth = 500 − 454 = 46.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: A data set of 10 values has a mean of 50. Nine of the values are 45, 47, 48, 49, 51, 52, 53, 54, 55. Find the tenth value.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 1) Total must be 10 × 50 = 500; the nine given add to 454; tenth = 500 − 454 = 46.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Create a data set of 6 numbers where the mode is 8, the median is 9 and the range is 10. Check your answer.

A shop’s salaries are: CEO 120,000; manager 45,000; and four workers 30,000, 28,000, 32,000, 30,000. Find the mean, median and mode. Which average would the CEO prefer to advertise, and which would the workers prefer?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Total = 120,000+45,000+30,000+28,000+32,000+30,000 = 285,000; mean = 285,000 ÷ 6 = 47,500; ordered 28k,30k,30k,32k,45k,120k → median (30k+32k)÷2 = 31,000; mode 30,000. The CEO would advertise the mean (47,500, the highest); the workers would prefer the median or mode (about 30,000–31,000), which…

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in The Range – Measuring the Spread.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: The range is not an average. Instead of telling us a typical value, it tells us how spread out the data is – how far apart the biggest and smallest values are. The range is the difference between the highest and lowest values. To find it: Example 1 – daily temperatures. Over five days near Kismaayo the midday temperatures were 15°C, 19°C, 23°C, 19°C, 16°C. The highest is 23°C and the lowest is 15°C, so the range is 23 − 15 = 8°C.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Comparing Data and Choosing the Right Average.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Now we bring everything together. When two sets of data are compared, looking at an average and the range together often reveals things that neither shows alone. Example – two footballers. Two players each score in six matches. Player A scores 12, 12, 13, 13, 14, 14 goals across a season’s games (mean of about 13). Player B scores 8, 10, 13, 14, 16, 19 (also a mean of about 13). Their means are the same, so on paper they look equal. But Player A’s range is 14 − 12 = 2, while Player B’s range is 19 − 8 = 11. Player…

selfAssessment: I can this unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, work through every example yourself on paper, and only move on when you feel confident. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you fresh practice questions, explain a tricky step in a new way, or check your working. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that still feels difficult.

I can why does this matter so much? We live in a world flooded with numbers. Shopkeepers in the Hargeisa market track their busiest hours; news reports quote average prices; sports commentators quote a player’s average score; weather services report average temperatures for planning trips between Mogadishu and Nairobi. People who understand averages can read these numbers wisely and make good decisions. People who do not can be misled – sometimes by accident, and sometimes on purpose. Learning to be a careful, critical thinker about data is one of the most useful skills mathematics can give you.

I can as you work, keep a pencil, some paper, and a calculator beside you. A calculator is allowed for the adding and dividing so that you can concentrate on the ideas – but you must still understand every step, because a calculator will happily give you a wrong answer if you press the wrong buttons. Mathematics is not a subject you learn only by reading; you must do it. If you get an answer wrong, that is not a failure – it simply shows you exactly what to practise next.
I can find the mode – the most common value – and recognise data that is bimodal (has two modes) or has no mode.
I can find the median – the middle value when the data is put in order – for both odd and even numbers of values.
I can calculate the mean by adding all the values and dividing by how many there are.
I can calculate the range – the difference between the highest and lowest values – and use it to describe how consistent data is.
I can compare two sets of data using averages and range together.
